items.title: Final Fantasy XIV opens up pre-orders for its fourth Eorzean Symphony album
items.content:
There can be no doubt in anyone’s mind that Final Fantasy XIV is a game that loves its music, and the most recent orchestral concert in Japan back in December 2025 is proof of that. But don’t you want to enjoy that orchestral music yourself despite lacking the ability to travel back in time and space to hear the concert live? Good news; that’s what the fourth Eorzean Symphony album is all about , which can be pre-ordered in physical form or downloaded from numerous musical services now.

Fans who pre-order the physical version will gain access to two Orchestrion rolls playing the orchestral versions of songs in-game, which may be more than enough additional draw for some fans . Meanwhile, everyone can enjoy some behind-the-scenes footage just below (although you should be aware ahead of time that it’s a lengthy watch at 26 minutes). Just in time for fans to start pining for new music when the next fan festival happens at the end of July, isn’t it?

items.title: Choose My Adventure: My return to the fascinating and fun Pictomancer job in Final Fantasy XIV
items.content:
“Try wielding different weapons and approaching fights with new tactics. Not because you must master them all, but because it will grant insight and context for those you would seek to protect.”

The words of her gunblade tutor echoed in the Roegadyn’s mind as she made her way back home long enough to change her gear; it was one of the few times the grizzled old Hrothgar wasn’t actively annoyed by his agreement to train her. Once more, she pondered her array of weapons until her ice blue eyes fell upon a massive paintbrush of magiteknical design. She was not particularly skilled with traditional aetheric manipulation, but the hardware had helped her overcome most of those shortcomings.

Rolling her shoulders, she picked up the odd weapon, which effectively amounted to a massive paint sprayer attached to a command board that served as its associated palette. Time to give this a try.

It has been a very long time since I’ve touched the Pictomancer class in Final Fantasy XIV . Long enough that, apparently, my hotbars weren’t set up and a gear set wasn’t in place, likely because I deleted it some time ago.

But hey, that’s what this month’s series is about: trying new-to-me things. And yes, the Pictomancer did feel new to me enough that I quite literally had to figure things out from scratch. At level 93.

That might sound like a bit of a daunting task, but considering that I was going to be throwing myself into the single-player trust dungeons, I was only going to be letting myself down if I messed things up and not my teammates. Plus, these always feel like the closest to a “live fire exercise” when I’m not able to get friends gathered together.

It’s just as well that I spammed the solo dungeons because I was tripping myself up quite a bit. The Pictomancer isn’t necessarily what I would call terribly complex, but it also has some of the most unique resource mechanics of FFXIV by a long shot in my opinion. At least when it comes to the throes of combat.

Initially I was focused on working out the best basic rotation possible, which seemed to mostly be about getting to a point when I would swap from basic spellcasting mode to subtractive palette mode, all while trying to gauge when would be the best time to fire my Moogle laser or put down the pretty field of empowering flowers.

Yes, for those unfamiliar, those are actual combat abilities. So is rainbow rail gun. This class is a trip.

Eventually I felt reasonably comfortable enough with what I figured were the basics, though during more protracted fights I was having some trouble working out best practices. More time than not, it seemed as if I was putting down my empowering field at the worst possible times despite knowing the boss fights I was facing. Maybe that’s not unique to the caster DPS experience, though. Black Mages seem to have that same issue.

The wrinkles deepened further both in fights and in my brain when I got to level 96 and unlocked the Madeen portion of my rotation. Suddenly getting to the fluffy kitty beast (seriously) felt like something I should regularly strive for, even if using the abilities to get there took longer while in combat than out-of-combat.

Over time, repeated dungeon runs, and some deaths, I feel as though I started to work it out. The hammer abilities and the white paint abilities, both of which can be cast while moving, are situational. The regular casts and the Moogle to Madeen route are foundational. It should be woven into my normal spellcasting pathing.

I’m hardly what one would call good at the Pictomancer. With the addition of the Madeen portrait, I often found myself forgetting where I was in my ability rotation, where I was standing, or both, which saw me either eat floor and start the boss fight over or just stop casting altogether, which would make me a bad DPS in a group setting. But even so, this class is still one of the most fascinating and distinct things I’ve played, either in FFXIV or in other MMORPGs. How often do you see whimsy used as a weapon?

I think the thing that makes the job more interesting to me is its middle ground between Black Mage complexity and simple damage mechanics, all while it has a colorful and fun-loving aesthetic and resource management that made me have to think. Though I will admit that its bubbling sound effects were getting kind of grating after a time.

items.title: Breaking Brick Mountains Returns on June 25!
items.summary: The collaboration event with Dragon Quest X, Breaking Brick Mountains, returns Thursday, June 25! Battle against gargantuan golems from the Dragon Quest series, once again making their way to Eorzea! Don't miss this opportunity to earn rich
items.content:
The collaboration event with Dragon Quest X, Breaking Brick Mountains, returns Thursday, June 25! Battle against gargantuan golems from the Dragon Quest series, once again making their way to Eorzea! Don't miss this opportunity to earn rich rewards, including the king slime crown, thug's mug, and the newly added Dragon Quest X framer's kit. * This event is identical to the one previously held. * If you have already completed the event, you can replay the quests via the seasonal event replay feature. For details, please check the special site.

Event Duration

Thursday, June 25, 2026 at 1:00 a.m. (PDT) / 8:00 (GMT) / 9:00 (BST) / 18:00 (AEST) to Monday, July 13, 2026 at 7:59 a.m. (PDT) / 14:59 (GMT) / 15:59 (BST) /14 July at 0:59 (AEST)

How to Participate

Begin the quest "Breaking Brick Mountains" by speaking to Havak Alvak of the Mythril Eye in Ul'dah – Steps of Nald (X:12.1 Y:8.2). During this event, players can purchase a King Slime Crown by speaking with the Toughie in Ul'dah – Steps of Nald (X:8.2 Y:12.2).

Rewards

Visit the special site for more details.

items.title: Wisdom of Nym: Speculating on Final Fantasy XIV Evercold’s weekly activity system
items.content:
So one of the very specific things I said when I was talking about Final Fantasy XIV’s last fan festival was cautioning people about getting out over their skis regarding the weekly activity system that’s coming in Evercold . We currently know very, very little about it from the base level, and thus it is a bad idea to speak too confidently about any of it because… we know very little about it. Presumably we’ll learn more in July. But here I am, writing a column in which I am knowingly getting out over my skis a bit.

In part, this is a deliberate maneuver because I am writing this as much as anything to demonstrate the known unknowns. But it’s also because I feel like doing a bit of speculating and seeing how close to the intent I actually manage to hit. So let’s talk about it, and the first thing I’m going to do is say that the most obvious source we have for the activity system is not something we should actually use as a source.

One of the slides from Yoshida’s keynote specifically shows off a page full of weekly activities as a mockup, and this is the most obvious source for speculating about what the weekly system will look like. However, I am honestly pretty certain that it was never meant to be anything more than a mockup to show what the interface will look like, rather than any sort of actual sample. For example, one of its items is “Embark on an Ocean Fishing voyage,” and while that one is cut off at the bottom of the shot, we can see that it’s offering as many points as an instanced dungeon.

So if you’re using that image as your basis on values or expectations, you should not be. It is not accurate and honestly does not pretend that it is showing you anything beyond a mockup. It’s why it was shown once and then not again. It is a demonstration of what the interface will look like, not what it will be or what it will reward, and thus nothing that is on there should be taken in absolute terms.

Where, then, do we start? Well, I would say that the smartest place to start is actually with our current system because that should give us some kind of baseline.

As it currently stands, players can earn 450 time-limited tomestones per week. If we assume that the players doing so are also earning weekly rewards as they are relevant elsewhere (and we pretend that you can wear two rings from the tomestone vendor, which you can’t, but bear with me), it takes a little over 12 weeks to get a full set of gear including your weapons from zero. So let’s make 12 weeks a baseline. This works pretty nicely, as it happens; there are 12 slots for you to equip something, after all, even if only one combat job reliably puts anything in the offhand slot, so it feels broadly “right” in this sense.

This broadly corresponds to the larger interface mockup, which features its milestones at increments of 3000 from what we can see. And I would go so far as to venture that we can expect, on a weekly basis, for the same basic rules to apply as they currently do, so five Expert dungeons in a week will get you to that milestone.

Keep in mind that at this point, we do not know what “expert” dungeons will look like if, indeed, they even still exist or use that name. Again, this is strictly about the basic shapes, and we haven’t been told that content itself is wildly changing so much as the ways in which we get rewards from it will change.

All of this is based on the assumption that our expected weekly activities will look the same as they do now if that’s how we choose to play. But it’s also assuming that our expected weekly activities are also confined to top-level play, and it’s here that I think there’s the possibility for something more interesting to go on. I just mentioned that we’re talking about “five Expert dungeons will cap you on rewards,” but there are two possible implementations here. The first – if point values stay the same – would be that “clear an Expert dungeon” is worth 600 points and can be repeated. But the second is that “clear an Expert dungeon,” “clear an instanced dungeon,” and “clear a max-level dungeon” are all individual objectives – or all of the aforementioned along with “clear a roulette” and “clear content with random party members.”

The thing is, doing an Expert dungeon would count for all of those activities. But it’s very possible for other things to count for several as well. If you’re doing leveling dungeons on other jobs, you’re still clearing instanced dungeons! And I suspect that’s part of the point.

While I can’t be certain at this time, my suspicion is that stacking different objectives is part of the end goal with this new system, but also in a way that doesn’t force you to choose quite as often. As it stands now, committing to level up a new job means that you have to do a lot of content on top of what you also will be doing in terms of endgame maintenance. Not an onerous burden by any means, but it does mean that if you have less time you have to choose between leveling or getting tomes.

I’m hopeful, albeit not certain, that one of the goals with weekly activity as a system is to change that. That’s not to say that it’ll be faster to level up another job than to just do Expert roulette, as I posited before; rather, it’s that if you’re already doing another job, you can get something out of it in terms of gear progress as well. You don’t have to do as many dungeons in a week to cap out your gear progression because it’s your overall activity that matters most.

That is an exciting prospect to me. As someone who generally engages with the game very broadly, I am fond of feeling like all of it feeds together more than it currently does. In some ways, it’s a mirror of something I’ve already expressed joy over when it comes to the game’s flexibility in its endgame. It is something that could use a refresh, and I’m hopeful to see something that promotes a healthy engagement with the game as a whole instead of a strict regimen – something that I think is broadly within the realm of possibility, obviously.

Or maybe it will just be “you can do your routine of experts all at once instead” and nothing will really change. I’ve been wrong before!

items.title: Sarah’s Final Fantasy XIV Fan Fest Experience
items.summary: What is the most magical event full of sparkle and wonder that only takes place every couple of years? Why, it's FFXIV FanFest! Sarah Ferries provides a journal of her experience at the event, including the Jollibee and Requiem Café visits.
items.content:
Let’s just start off by saying that the weather was way better in Los Angeles than the previous Fan Fests in Las Vegas. Yes, things like hotels were more expensive, but I would take that any day over the 100+-degree heat. Even Yoshi P (Producer and Director Naoki Yoshida) said he was melting!

I arrived about 8:30am for the 10am keynote to get a good seat. Just walking into the stage area, it felt a lot bigger than in previous years in Vegas. I was in my Pictomancer cosplay, which was a bit chilly, but better than being too hot. It did get warmer as the keynote went on, though. It truly was jam-packed and longer than a normal keynote. I was intending to post updates on social media like normal, but the internet was garbage inside the convention center so I just made notes on my phone, and was at least able to use Discord. That said, I also forgot to get the media-only WiFi information, so that’s partly on me.

Most of the keynote people loved. There was lots of cheering and clapping, especially with the Evercold trailer . However, when it came to showcasing the Reborn/Evolved modes, the people around me didn’t appear to like it, especially after Yoshi P announced that all new jobs will be only using the Evolved mode. I think it was the initial shock of changing things that really set people off, but after talking through it with people, it doesn’t seem like it’s going to be as big of a change as some people think. I was able to meet up with a lot of my friends from Twitch chats and Final Fantasy XIV, and after we all talked about it, I think it will be better. As someone who plays on a controller, this might be of particular benefit to me. I have my own rotation, and I have no idea if that’s the “correct” Pictomancer rotation, but with the evolved mode it might allow for more flexibility.

After the welcome ceremony, I went with a group of friends to Jollibee, which was holding a Final Fantasy XIV collaboration for Fan Fest. Even though the Roulette Game Show looked fun, we were all very hungry. We arrived at Jollibee and it fortunately wasn’t that busy. I think we only waited about a half hour. We wanted to go to this exact one, because we were told this was the only one that was fully decorated. I was amused at G’raha Tia being on the trash cans eating a sandwhich. This seemed to be the best time to go, since outside of Fan Fest people were saying that the lines at other times were many hours long. I got the $21 box to get my fancy emote. I only ate the sandwich there, but it was delicious. I really liked the sauce, and it came with the good reminder that no chocobos were harmed in the making of the food. We were wondering what they were going to do with the decorations after the event was over. I would’ve loved to take one of the aetheryte lights and put it in my room.

We made it back to Fan Fest around 3pm, and I wanted to see some of the developer panel to understand the Evolved mode a little better, but then I got distracted by shiny cosplayers and friends. Unfortunately, by around 4pm my feet hurt so bad I could not walk, which is partly the price I paid for wearing my cosplay shoes, and I ended up going back to my hotel to put on my sneakers before coming back. I was able to get back just in time to watch the “Glamoured to Life” cosplay walk for around twenty minutes before having to go to my meet and greet. I am glad I was able to see some of the streamers I watch walk, including my favorite scene I was able to see: Sultana drinking from the goblet on stage. The crowd reaction was great. She came out and pulled out the cup, at which point you could hear the gasps and the air leave the room. Then she went to drink, to which everyone immediately screamed, and then stood there for a second before giving a thumbs up. That got a lot of cheers.

When I got to my meet and greet, I am surprised how fast and easy it was. At bigger cons, these things are a mess and you often have to wait hours to meet your chosen guest. However, at Fan Fest you simply had to line up fifteen minutes before your appointment, and then go through relatively fast. I was able to meet Jason Charles Miller, who originally voiced Raubahn Aldynn in A Realm Reborn, but is perhaps better known for performing the vocals in the theme songs for Shadowbringers and Endwalker, plus “Unleashed” from Dawntrail . He was very nice and I even got to chat with him a little bit while my photo was taken.

The Piano Performance with Keiko and special guest Amanda Achen was out of this world. As always, Keiko has a beautiful princess dress for the performance. The sound and stage quality was fantastic. There were some camera issues, where the shot stayed on Keiko for a very long time even when Amanda was singing. I’m chalking that up to technical issues, hopefully. I’m so glad I was able to see the stage from my seat so I could see Amanda singing. Luckily, it wasn’t most of the concert, so I was able to watch the big screens on the sides of the stage.

I was able to attend a portion of “From Tacos to Tenders: Serving up the Lyrics of FFXIV ” with Michael-Christopher Koji Fox. The man is hilarious, and I love listening to him talk about the game, the antics, and how he comes up with lyrics. This panel showed how Square Enix really listens to its players as well as showcasing famous misheard lyrics to some of the songs from the game. One of the most misheard lyrics is from Ultima theme, with players chanting along with “big fat tacos” instead of “Beat, the Heart of Sabik.” Typing it out, it seems like a bizarre mishearing, but you’d have to hear it. He then had fans come up on stage to write new lyrics to “eScape”, which the audience voted on. The winning set of lyrics was actually changed for the Primals live concert at the end of the night! VTuber IronMouse was a special guest during this panel and gave a small concert at the end which was actually really good. I had never seen her before, but I liked her performance.

As much as I wanted to see random Q&As with Yoshi P, I hadn’t yet got the chance to play any of the games yet. I heard a lot of them were capping lines at 4:30, so wanted to get as much done as possible, though I am sad I missed the question about the butt slider.

I started with Run Alpaca Run, which is pretty much a carnival race horse game where you roll a ball into various holes: think of a mini version of Skee-Ball. I did not win, but it was still fun. I wasn’t able to get into the Crystalline Conflict Palaistra Challenge, as the line was already too long, and it’s not something I got into in-game, so it’s hard to compare the physical game to the digital area. I did try the Pelupelu Pitch, which is another carnival style game of ring toss. I am also terrible at this game and scored zero points, but it was still fun. Next was Mica’s Magical Maneuvers, which was a reaction-time based game. Sticks drop suddenly and you have to grab them before they hit the floor. Metem’s Limit Break Challenge was another carnival-style game I did not partake in, because of my tiny, sensitive hands. It gives participants one opening and a single punch to score big DPS; I watched a guy punch a score of 649, which was pretty high compared to the others. However, I did try the Arcadion Slam to try out for the Solution Nine Yans; shooting hoops with a basketball that couldn’t go wrong, right? Wrong! In my haste of trying to make baskets in competition with other people on a time limit, the ball bounced off the rim and hit me in the face. Luckily, it didn’t leave a bruise or a bloody nose, just hurt pride and some redness. Last but not least, Ride the Rhythm was the most popular game at Fan Fest. The line was always long, but it was definitely the highlight. This was basically a disco floor that people had to dance on, but with elements from a boss fight, complete with area of effect squares, tankbuster circles, and a stack marker. It was really cool to see boss mechanics from in the game come to life in person. There was one thing I didn’t get to do but I wish I had: the photo spot with G’raha Tia on a gondola. That was probably the second most popular thing at Fan Fest. I did get a photo at Aunt Tii’s Tacos in Tuliyollal, though.

Having Fan Fest modeled after the latest expansion, Dawntrail , and showcasing its main areas like Solution Nine and Tuliyollal was done really well. There were lots of photo opportunities with the giant “XIV” letters, well known landmarks like Aunt Tii’s Tacos, different Solution Nine areas such as a lounge and bar, and even a photo op with Honey B. Lovely, everyone’s favorite boss. (Alright, not everyone’s, but she was pretty popular.) The only thing I would’ve liked more of was an expanded Solution Nine lounge with more couches and chairs, as they were always full of people, meaning many had to sit along the walls or on the floor.

The last hurrah of the evening was a concert from The Primals, the official band of Final Fantasy XIV lead by Sound Director Masayoshi Soken since 2014. The members are Masayoshi Soken, Michael-Christopher Koji Fox, GUNN, Eikichi Iwai, and Tetsuya Tachibana. Last Fan Fest, people brought their own light sticks for the Primals concert and this year Square Enix decided to sell them at their merch station, which made it that much more fun and I was able to get one of the last light sticks before they sold out. There were multiple colors to choose from on the light stick to match the songs the Primals were playing, and everyone in the crowd seemed to be on the same page. The concert began with the “checking member status” display on the video screens, as one would see waiting for a dungeon to fill in game. The band began “Open Sky”, followed by “Scream”, “Ride the Rhythym”, “Not Afraid” (with special guest Yoshi P), “Unleashed”, “Shadowbringers”, “Absolute Tyranny”, “A Long Fall”, “Rise”, “eScape”, and finally “Under the Weight”. I was surprised when the concert ended as I was enjoying it so much I wasn’t ready for it to end.

Before I left Anaheim, I was able to visit the FFXIV Requiem Café. There was not a lot of merch left, and all the pastries and some of the drinks were sold out. I was able to grab a New Adventures drink, which had a sprout bookmark in the whipped cream atop a refreshing mint citrus soda, and the Scree! ice cream drink. This was a brown sugar milk tea with chocolate drizzle topped with a toasted marshmallow cold foam, gold lustre dust, and a serpent gummy. Both of these were VERY delicious. The inside of the café was magical and wondrous, with a fake giant tree that looked like a weeping willow with a few seats inside. It had a TV showing some story quests, as well as many cardboard stand ups of our favorite scions. The store was decked out with gaming computers that had the free trial loaded up for people to play the game, along with a post-it wall for gamers to leave their mark. I wish I could take the tree with me and put it in my house!

Overall this year’s Fan Fest was yards above the Las Vegas one in 2023. The weather was much nicer, there was a bigger space that was laid out much better, and there were better food options. As always, thank you Square Enix for putting on these events!

items.title: Choose My Adventure: Cutting through the latest normal raids of Final Fantasy XIV Dawntrail
items.content:
She braced through the buzzing strain of the gunblade’s explosive strike, the slashing of steel and roar of fire just covering the monster’s bellow of pain. The beast retaliated almost immediately, swinging with the blind rage borne of agony at her. She barely fired off a magical shell from the weapon that brought up a half dome of aether, taking the brunt of the clubbing swing, though some still managed to push through.

A soft, familiar glow of energy swiftly enveloped her, injecting new vigor into weary sinew and encouraging her to push on. Her strikes paired with the magical burst from the red-haired Miqo’te just behind and the wild and dusty-looking Hyur who looked for all the world like a whirlwind of matched swords.

The thrill of battle swelled back into the Roegadyn’s heart. She remembered why she even thought to take up this path. At least for a brief moment. There was a fight to win, after all.

Before I recount my time in Final Fantasy XIV’s most recent raids, I wanted to offer some thanks to those who provided words of encouragement. Things locally are still not better, but they were improved enough that I was ready to get back in to this week’s tasks, and those who did leave some messages did a lot to buoy me.

What also helped me push in to these encounters was having some good friends tag along, namely my husband and a mutual pair of friends. I always seem to find more courage when I do new-to-me things with familiar people around. So it was that we started first with the Arcadion.

For context to regular players, I got as far as M7 when the series was more current, but the Brute Abombinator proved to be something of a wall for me and mine at the time. Of course, increased item levels and more knowledge of the fight meant that this return trip was much smoother. Still, I admit to some trepidation about going back in, but I’m glad to report that getting my face caved in as frequently as I had previously meant I also had some muscle memory to fall back on.

From there, it continued to move fairly smoothly as the rest of the sci-fantasy pro wrestlers juiced up with monstrous souls came one by one. Each fight was better than the last, while Tyrant proved to be a stern challenge. And then at the Lindwurm things kind of… I don’t want to say petered out, but it was certainly less taxing by comparison. Narratively speaking, it was a bit of a victory lap, but it still had fun mechanics.

Of course, where this series of fights really shined was in the vibe. I used to be a big pro wrestling fan and still dabble now and again to this day, so watching the whole thing play out was really a treat. And I cannot possibly write about this raid series without noting the music. Every song was awesome, but personal props definitely go to Sinister and The Mirror .

Once I got through those fights without too much damage, I felt my confidence grow fairly significantly. Even when I was trying to beat myself down, the people who joined me made sure to talk me away from self-flagellation, kept me in check, and reminded me of context. This was all-new to me. I was doing fine. Nobody else is mad at me.

It’s really easy to just ignore that advice, but it was really heartening to be reminded in the moment. Some days it’s easier than others to forget that most people in FFXIV aren’t judgmental little goblins. Someone in the PUG chat even welcomed me in to a new fight at one point.

The following day, we went in to the Vana’diel raids. As I mentioned before, I had at least unlocked The Second Walk, and going in I once more felt a little bit of creeping worry, particularly since I was the main tank for my third of the 24-player team. At least during the bosses I was able to just be a DPS with sterner defenses.

These raids were, by and large, a bit of a stiffer test for me, particularly the final fight in The Second Walk and the penultimate fight in The Third Walk. In the case of the former, I at least could kind of understand what it was that I was doing wrong, but the latter kind of baffled me. I had to be scraped off of the floor multiple times. Six, to be exact. My penchant for self-deprecation made sure to keep track.

Once again, I’m glad I had the support I did, both direct and indirect. For example, pretty much my entire portion of the raid team and several others in the whole squad were being slapped around, and one of the helpers was definitely quiet and tense because, like, this dude was freaking hard. I’m at least willing to practice more and try again. Especially if it means I get that emote.

While the vibes of the Arcadion series were immaculate, the whole feeling through Echoes of Vana’diel was one of general indifference to me. This is not to suggest that I’m going to discount Final Fantasy XI out of hand, especially since it was a formative game that really got me in to MMORPGs. More than anything, though, the storyline that wove through the whole thing just felt a bit throwaway.

items.title: Wisdom of Nym: Speculating on Final Fantasy XIV Evercold’s new tank job
items.content:
As it stands right now, Final Fantasy XIV has an interesting… I don’t want to say conundrum , precisely, but situation with its tank jobs. We know that out of the four currently in the game, three of them will be designated as off-tanks within the tank role, while Paladin and one other will be the main tanks. This means nothing inside of four-person groups, I assume, but it might mean something in full-group content, although I imagine it’ll mean less than one might think much of the time. (The game doesn’t break if a Scholar and a Sage are paired by the duty finder.)

The result is that there are a huge amount of variables to be considered when it comes to what the new tank job, and the only things we really know at this point is that it will not be a job from the series history. All we have to go on right now is a memory of a Garlean praefectus holding two shields. So what might it be?

I mean, this is the obvious derivation from the hint, right? Rhitahtyn uses two shields. Our new tank job is going to use two shields. This would be kind of the most obvious thing possible.

While I’m being a little flippant, there is something to this insofar as it would be an obvious sort of tank job as well as sending a clear message about what makes a main tank over an off tank. Having main tank jobs both have a shield creates a distinction, and it also gives us access to something that is visually distinct. And let’s face it, paired shields as a combat style is cool.

So what’s arguing against it? Two things. First of all, the way that FFXIV is currently coded, shields are offhand weapons and there’s no way to put a main hand in the off hand or vice versa. You could, of course, create a whole new category of mainhand shields or create a new set of paired shields, but that feels inherently like cheating where you have shields that don’t count as shields. The other thing is that Yoshida loves turning his shirt into a hint. But this wouldn’t be a hint. This is just telling us the new job. I realize that, for example, the Batman shirt made it pretty clear we were getting Dark Knight, but I suspect he would try a little harder. Especially when he could have just worn or made a different shirt.

You know who Rhitahtyn paired up with a bunch? Nero. And what does Nero use in combat? A hammer. In fact, the one time we’ve seen Rhitahtyn’s gunshield reused, it was for Grynewaht, who primarily used a big dang hammer in battle. And let’s be real, a big smacking hammer is absolutely the sort of thing that would make sense for a new job and isn’t currently in the game.

Frankly, a lot of the pieces fit here. Big maces are, in fact, a pretty standard weapon, to the point that it’s kind of weird FFXIV has found the space for multiple flavors of sword, a paintbrush, and flying syringes before hammers. But “big hammer” has also not historically been a thing that a job employed in the franchise. There have been a handful of characters who used hammers and a few moments where they’ve cropped up, but more often it has been around the fringes. Yes, we have a couple of hammer skins elsewhere, but that’s also true of scythes, so that’s hardly an impediment.

There are, of course, arguments against this. First of all, this does mean that it’s harder to necessarily tell the difference between a main tank and an off-tank if the new main tank also is wielding a big smacky weapon. Second, just because a weapon exists in the abstract doesn’t mean it needs to be a new job; maces have often been the province of healing jobs in this series, so there’s not necessarily a reason for that to change.

I mean, split the difference, right? I just finished explaining the logical connection here, you just head up to Grynewaht and then stop. This one is so obvious I don’t really need to do any more explanation, but it also solves the aforementioned issue of shields being a separate thing and any problems with having shields as main hands. Problem solved, right?

Well… no, not quite, because I actually think this is less likely than either of the prior options for a few reasons. The first is because of Beastmaster, which we know is going to use an axe and a shield. Now, I don’t mind the idea that we have a melee DPS job using a shield at all, but I do think it’s unlikely that we’d get a new job using a shield and another thing three months before we get another job with a shield and another thing. At least dual shields is very visually distinct from Paladin or Beastmaster; a hammer and a shield is not.

More reasons? Yeah, actually. First of all, “hammer and shield” does have a history in the series, most often being a particular weapon combination for White Mages. But there is a game where a tanky job has a hammer and shield, and that’s Final Fantasy III , where the Viking job has just that combo. But we already know that these new jobs are both original to the series. If this expansion had a reason to call a job “Viking,” why would it not . That makes no sense.

“Is Garlean armor power armor,” the most epic argument in forum history, locked after 14,378 pages of furious debate. If we assume that it is, though, there’s certainly room to go all in on that.

Let’s be real, part of what makes Garlean bosses cool is also what makes power armor cool conceptually. Iron Man, as a character, does not have defined powers beyond some broad-strokes stuff. “Oh, I have lasers and missiles and cool gadgets that work to solve this particular combat problem” sounds like a cheat until it happens and it looks cool. Frankly, even Rhitahtyn’s actual boss fight is basically this. It’s lasers and explosions and crap all over the place because he'[s the boss. This wouldn’t be the worst theme for a tank job.

The problem, of course, is that every tank job in FFXIV needs a main-hand weapon, and I struggle to think of one that would fit for these purposes. Not to mention that there are a whole lot of fending suits that are not in any way resembling of any kind of powered armor, and you still need to make them make sense. I wouldn’t be upset if this happens, but I’m not betting on it.

items.title: FINAL FANTASY RESONANCE is available for pre-purchase now!

items.content:
https://store.steampowered.com/app/3259780/ What if FINAL FANTASY had continued to push the limits of pixel art and evolved from its classic roots? The first FINAL FANTASY to join the HD‑2D lineup, FINAL FANTASY RESONANCE delivers a visually striking experience that feels both nostalgic and new, enhanced by dynamic camera angles and pixelized 3D models that make every moment more dramatic and immersive. Return to the pixel‑art world of FINAL FANTASY, evolved from its classic roots. Travel with companions bound by fate and call on Visions to aid you in battle to uncover the truth behind a force that threatens to bring everything to ruin.

Digital Deluxe Edition:

This product is a bundle that includes the FINAL FANTASY RESONANCE base game along with the Magitek & Grimoire Deluxe Pack, which contains an item that transforms your chocobo's appearance into magitek armor, an item exclusive to this pack that unlocks a certain character's Fel Meteor Limit Burst, and useful items to aid you at the start of your journey. Digital Deluxe Edition Contents: ▼ FINAL FANTASY RESONANCE Base Game ▼ Magitek & Grimoire Deluxe Pack *Images are for illustrative purposes only. Actual in‑game visuals may differ. 　 Pack Contents: 　 • Magitek Armor Key x1 　 • Archwitch’s Grimoire x1 　 • Mastery Ring x1 (Increases MRP gained in battle by a certain amount) 　 • Tent x5 　 • Mist Potion x5 　 • Mist Ether x5 　 • Phoenix Down x5 　 • Remedy x3

Pre-order Bonus: Magitek Airship & Squire Starter Pack

items.title: Choose My Adventure: How the week from hell stole my time in Final Fantasy XIV
items.content:
After a morning stretch exercise session and time in a nice warm bath, the Roegadyn was feeling closer to her old self. Or at the very least, more like a functional person in regular society, anyway. But then she would move to her weapons locker and realize that she wasn’t really all that typical.

Her eyes fell on the gunblade, a slimmer version of what was otherwise a far larger and more violent type of weapon, and already she could feel the buzzing in her palms that its explosive vibrations cause. She had gotten training from an old master of its techniques, and while she was at least capable with it, she never really got over how it felt.

Still, today was meant to be the day that she tried again. With an exhalation from her nose, she gripped the handle and unseated it from its hook.

I’ll come clean with all of you, this week has not been very good to me. A fair bit of it was for personal reasons that I’ll not get in to here (nobody wants to read that anyway), but another contributing factor was how busy the week in general was in a few other facets as well. In short, my time in Final Fantasy XIV for this week was pretty much cut off at the knees a bit. But in the interest of disclosure and in cataloguing my time in the game for this column, both good and bad, I figure I should at least detail what happened and a bit of why.

First off, the work that came up over the course of this week was quite a bit. Not as much as others, surely, but it was already cutting in to a fair bit of what I have set aside for myself and for projects. This thing that I do is just as much about time management as anything, which isn’t a skill I’ve been very good at developing, so that was making an existing problem a bit worse.

Then there was my old nemesis: tanxiety . I have mentioned previously that I enjoy the hell out of the Gunbreaker class, and that isn’t a lie, but every now and again the tank job’s importance suddenly crashes down on me like a giant pile of bricks. Even if my role in a eight-player raid would end up being off-tank, there’s still that expectation. And of course, entering the 24-player raid means I’m the prime tank. My resolve faltered.

And finally there are those personal matters that sprang up. It’s nothing very serious, I will say at least, but there really was a lot of things happening in my personal sphere that ensured my mind wasn’t right, or that I did other things, or redirected focus. I refer you all again to the aforementioned bad time management problem.

Yet for all of my personal failings heading into this project this week, I still do want to get through it and into the task that’s been asked of me by the polls. I at least managed to do the quest stuff leading into The Second Walk of the Vana’diel raid, and I know for a fact that I’ve got backup to call on. I just couldn’t do it this time.

I’d like to think that a lot of MMORPG players run into this issue, whether one has a lot of time to themselves or not. Still, I do have something of a literal job to do in a few different senses of the word, and so all I can ask for is grace and patience and belief that I am going to get past this and move on. That was a lot of what the FFXIV’s MSQ has been about, after all.

So yes, I will do it next time. I’m going to do it next time. It’s a question of not if but which one I take on first. And so inspiration strikes to ask that of you all.

Which of the current raids should I tackle first?

Polling will wrap up at the usual 1:00 p.m. EDT time on Friday, June 12th. In the meantime, I’ve got to take a moment or two to get my mind right, get priorities in order, and just… do it. Oftentimes throwing myself into the fire is the fastest way for me to cook.

items.title: Wisdom of Nym: The crafting-heavy content of Final Fantasy XIV’s patch 7.51
items.content:
I’ve talked a lot during Final Fantasy XIV ‘s current expansion cycle about the way that the game’s developers have broken down patches into numerous smaller parts. Patch 7.51 is, at least to me, an interesting example of where that particular path runs into some issues. If I look at it from my own perspective as someone who broadly engages with the entirety of the game but also doesn’t have any particular need to prove that I am a very strong digital manly-man who can beat the very hardest of video game bosses, this patch does contain A Content. It contains several contents, even!

However, not everyone likes crafting and gathering. That’s not just a permitted mode of play in the game but an expected one. Some people do not care for crafting or gathering. What do you do for those particular people? Well, you give them content that is not centered around crafting or gathering. But in this case, doing that involves… well, proving that you are a strong digital manly-man. That’s sort of it.

Now, in broad strokes I could say that clearly, the real answer here is that we need to not have Ultimates, but that’s not really the problem here. We do have combat content on deck. We’ve got Beastmaster and the remainder of Occult Crescent both on the way. The thing is that this content all needs to go somewhere, and there is no actual optimal way to break it up that can’t wind up leading to the same problems.

As an example, let’s say that you put Occult Crescent here and you completely ignore that the last stage of weapons will ensure that the weapons from the Ultimate are purely decorative. You don’t have to care about that. That means you have purely non-tryhard combat content for two months… and then it means you don’t have anything for July’s patch. “Let’s move Beastmaster up, then.” All right, now you have nothing for September’s patch. You can make the content come faster, but it just means moving when you deal with the problem.

I don’t even think it’s necessarily a problem in the particularly grim sense; it’s just a reality that this content has to go somewhere, and no matter what you will have a certain level of resentment if you don’t engage in this content. At least this way the improved crafting kit can help you fund your Ultimate raiders if you want to?

Fortunately for me, I am not resistant to this content. Perhaps less fortunately for me, our newest stretch of Cosmic Exploration is broadly the same as all of our prior stages of Cosmic Exploration, which is not shocking but still not exactly as engaging as I’d like. Do the missions, upgrade your tools; there’s too much land here for too little actual content, and I really hope that Evercold takes advantage of the fact that we now know how much land can actually be included in an expansion. (Seriously, in this expansion we’ve gotten six new zones in patches.)

Are there good reasons to not have those zones filled up with FATEs and quests and such? Yes. Are there reasons that they don’t have hunt targets? Yes. Are they rigged for flying? No, which probably reduces some elements of design. But there’s so much real estate here and you can honestly barely ever leave the base camp while getting access to most of what Auxesia has to offer. That shouldn’t work this way.

I will say that unlike the targets of my earlier criticisms, Auxesia has at least worked hard to have a unique and charming visual feel. The first two zones were unnecessarily boring, and Oizys was not boring but took a little time to really come into its own. Auxesia’s ruined civilization appearance kicks in right from the start, and I enjoy that fact. I would enjoy it even more if I believed the story that has been teased about the lost civilization was going to actually pay off in any way, shape, or form, but alas…

What I will say is that I broadly like the one new addition in the form of mastery missions, which are fun freeform challenges once you have the best gear out there. They’re optional, and you don’t really need to do them for most of the rewards you would want, which is a good thing, but they solve the existing problem of earning the fully upgraded tools and then not really having any reason to keep working on the area. Now you have an ongoing reason and something to chase, and it does actually play into making the content and the climb of power relevant in the longer term.

Also, I am really glad that we have now had three expansions doing the crafter relic quests, and I definitely feel this was the most painless version. Points for that, too.

Meanwhile, the custom deliveries are… custom deliveries. I don’t want to sound dismissive or anything because I always enjoy these particular options, but they have not meaningfully evolved in a long time, and the devs do not seem interested in any real evolution. It’s content to do for the next few weeks, followed inevitably by “and now it fades into the background except when you’re leveling or need that boost in scrips.” Which may not even be a thing in Evercold , so who knows.

I have very little opinion on the Ultimate beyond “it’s very funny that some of the more toxic influencers got banned right before they got to do progress, isn’t it weird how these groups overlap, wonder what that says” and “it’s actually great that Kefka trolls people,” but if you wanted those opinions, there they are.

Overall, it feels like a very filler-y patch in what we already know is a filler bit of time. Above and beyond the fact that it’s here to slowly walk out the content that we’re going to be experiencing until the expansion launches in January, this is content that is meant to provide something new until the next fan festival hits in late July. It does provide that. Does it feel particularly graceful or comprehensive in that regard? I suppose it might if I were far more invested in the story of Cosmic Exploration than I actually am, but I suspect that is a minority audience anyway.

Still, to a certain extent that’s also complaining that there’s a toy in your Happy Meal. I knew what I was getting into, and it fills that slot. That’s patch 7.51. It’s what it says on the tin. I kinda wish it said something different on the tin, maybe, but I think some of that is not actually a solvable problem, and some of the rest is ultimately complaining about shows I don’t watch. So it goes!

items.title: Patch 7.51 Notes

Patch 7.51 introduces the seventh installment of the ultimate raid series, Dancing Mad (Ultimate), as well as new custom deliveries featuring Tiisol Ja. And for our intrepid Disciples of the Hand and Land, new exploration awaits on Auxesia. * Additional features will be implemented in future updates:

items.title: Choose My Adventure: A dip back in to Final Fantasy XIV’s untouched content pond
items.content:
The Roegadyn groaned, stirring beneath the satiny sheets of her bed as wakefulness slowly crept back in to her body. Her forearm draped over her eyes, shielding her from the perceived morning sun despite her basement bedroom not having any windows. Blood flow slowly swelled into ache. Resolution had started as regret.

She had grown indolent and apathetic these past couple of months, but she had finally met the limit of patience with being idle and steeled herself the night before to reinvigorate herself. To return to form. To activity. To life.

But damn if this first step wasn’t the hardest.

I will fully admit that I’m using this month’s series of Choose My Adventure columns for wholly selfish reasons. You see, Final Fantasy XIV is my home MMORPG, but these past few weeks I’ve pushed it far to the back burner. I’ve kept pace with the MSQ of course, but a lot of what I liked doing in the game had been set aside due to multiple personal issues. Not just content things, either; social events and roleplay have also fallen by the wayside.

But with this one winning out in the polls (just barely), I’m hoping that this will give me the motivation I seek to get back into the swing of things. I do miss this game quite a bit and a lot of the connections I’ve built through it, and it would be nice to strengthen those some more.

But first things first. I had an otter backpack to get .

When I have peeked back into the game again for MSQ things or the occasional idle roulette run, I had settled on Gunbreaker as my new main job. Tanking has also been something I had missed doing in XIV and the class just feels so damn good to play. But then I also was thinking about other loved (and max level) classes, specifically the Dancer and Viper. So I took several moments to reacclimate to both, seeing if I still had rotational muscle memory.

The Viper, unsurprisingly, came extremely easy considering how smoothly the class flows and the fact that it was my main choice through all of base Dawntrail. The Dancer, meanwhile, still ended up feeling a bit awkward to me despite it aligning with my character and playstyle vibe so well previously. Still, it was nice to pull that out of mothballs.

In the end, though, I elected to get a run in as a Viper for a quick rust-removing shakedown. I ran through the MSQ duty, landing on Castrum Meridianum. Unsurprisingly, it all went extremely smooth. Myself and one of the other members of the PUG openly reminisced about the dungeon’s old ARR version, both the good and the bad. The game’s community, broadly speaking, continues to be FFXIV’s greatest asset.

Most of what I otherwise did in this return was socialize. I took some time to pull together a new outfit or two. I chatted some friends up who were online. I idled around in my in-game house for a bit. The night later closed with attending an arm wrestling tournament in a friend’s roleplay venue. I know that last one sounds boring as hell on paper, but the bantering among other visitors and especially the combatants was top notch.

I didn’t really do much in terms of actual progress, but I did feel reminded about why I call this MMORPG home. It felt a bit like pulling the tarp off of a well-loved classic car and hitting the road again purely for the sake of enjoying the drive. Yeah, this game still does a lot for me.

The question then is what the main things to do from this point forward are because I’ve let quite a lot of side content fall fallow.

I haven’t completely avoided all of the new things that have been introduced post-Dawntrail, but I had kind of set much of those activities aside owing to the aforementioned personal matters. But now, I think that it is time to get on some of those things. I know I’ve got plenty of time between now and Evercold, but it still would feel good to get some of those things off of my checklist. Thus, poll one:

The next question is about classes yet again. As I brought up earlier, there are a few jobs that I’ve got to cap, all of which are at varying item levels, so a little bit of grinding (or gil spending) is possible, but I don’t mind doing any one of them, honestly. So let’s narrow that down too.

items.title: Wisdom of Nym: What will Final Fantasy XIV’s next ranged DPS job be?
items.content:
You may be wondering how I can possibly speculate on our next two jobs when our hints come down to a single t-shirt and a statement that neither of the two new jobs we’re getting in Final Fantasy XIV’s expansion will be from elsewhere in the series. To which I say… in a way, that kinda makes life easier! The information plane is narrower, and I don’t have to speculate about much beyond the nature of the weaponry, which is explicitly not based on prior jobs.

Now, that doesn’t mean it can’t be based on characters elsewhere in the series, but it does mean that our next ranged DPS is very specifically not going to be a job we have already seen elsewhere. Thus, I have assembled a list of potential weaponry and concepts that could potentially fit. Which one will be accurate? We’ll find out in July or at least get some more hints then, I assume. Let’s get going.

First and foremost, let’s start with something obvious that I’ve brought up before. Our boy on Yoshida’s shirt did have dual guns (dual gun-shields, sure, but dual guns) and “two pistols” is a combat style several NPCs have used for ages, including Merlwyb. It is an obvious angle wherein we do not have it in the game but we do have a lot of evidence it exists across the world, which makes it something to potentially be worked in.

What has long been the primary argument against this has been the idea that Machinist exists and has the “gun” side of things sewn up, but there are two elements that should be considered here. First of all, Machinist is increasingly not a case of splitting itself between “gun” and “engineering.” The job is about machines and gadgets with guns as the backup, just like how Ninja and Viper derive from similar series sources but have different elements. Second, while that argument definitely held up a bit when figuring out things like starting from a base class and moving to an advanced job, it’s less of an issue when you can just start with the job – something that has also been the case now for every single expansion since the first.

Of course, you do have to come up with an actual combat style for the job that feels distinct from Machinist in its execution, and that itself could be more of a challenge, but I definitely feel like it has more legs and existing infrastructure to prop it up than most of the other options. Can you make the job different enough from Machinist to still work? I certainly think so without much difficulty. It is possible the team feels otherwise.

We’ve all acknowledged at this point that there are Norse inspirations in Evercold’s background, and at this point we associate Norse folks with throwing stuff. Axes and hammers, especially. It would be remiss of me not to mention that a hatchet in both hands as the Au Ra runs at you and starts tossing them would certainly make sense as a new job, and it is definitely does not have a series precedent!

I feel there’s also something to consider here which is that the idea of a very Viking-like job would provide some useful added flavor for another ranged job. One of the things that currently exists is that you have two jobs which are both primarily about buffing allies (Dancer is a very buff-focused job, while Bard is lightly buff-focused) and then you have Machinist. It is possible for Machinist and this job to be more focused on debuffs, with Machinist being a bit lighter and this one being more intent on debuffs. Bringing back Break-style abilities with a pair of tomahawks? That tracks.

The major argument against this would be twofold. First of all, it’s unrelated to Rhitahtyn in any way, shape, or form. Second, the broader idea of throwing weapons has mostly been adapted by Dancer; throwing axes is a Warrior thing, and one-handed axes are already getting a new job when Beastmaster rolls out. It feels like this might be a bit too much reuse.

I have been scrupulous in avoiding saying with the prior two options that we are going to have some overlap no matter what in terms of ranged weapons, because lo, the mighty crossbow remains! Not a gun, not a bow, we’ve long seen crossbows represented with ranged limit breaks but we have yet to wield one ourselves – and let’s remember that two-handed swords and staves are also used in limit breaks, so that hardly precludes their addition.

Not only do crossbows have ample series history (they’ve appeared in every Ivalice game, and we know Yoshida loves those), there’s plenty of space to make lots of versions of them for varied designs. In fact, there are even some weaponskills related to them and guns from Final Fantasy XI , and in that game status-based bolts are the main reason to use crossbows over higher-damage guns! Again, this is unconnected to Rhitahtyn, but there is some room for crossbows…

…if you can get around their being just crossbows.

The main problem crossbows have here is that while they do appear fairly often, they rarely matter . Crossbows in the games where they exist are either something you use for specific scenarios or because you have no better options. FFXI players never seek out crossbows. Heck, this even mirrors the real-life issue of crossbows; they’re even more functionally obsolete than a bow and arrow, being basically guns that are harder to load and fire. In FFXIV , where guns are commonplace, those guns already fill the role of “weapon you don’t need extensive training to make reasonable ranged attacks with” over needing another stopgap. I wouldn’t be shocked to see a crossbow job, sure, but I also see them as perhaps less likely by the very nature of crossbows.

So there’s actually a remarkably long history of whips in the franchise! Like, it’s not All Whips All The Time, but we get a fair number of whips. And while I just took crossbows to task for being kinda boring, the thing is that whips are never boring. Whips are cool enough that every single time I play Elden Ring I try to say that this time I’m not going to be using whips and then no, I use whips.

The advantages here are obvious. For one thing, the franchise history means there are lots of whip-adjacent job fragments to pull from. Seven from Final Fantasy Type-0 , for example, has an assortment of abilities that could be expanded into a proper job here. For another, there are lots of different styles of whips that have different visual attributes to help differentiate them.

items.title: The Make It Rain Campaign Begins May 29!
items.summary: The Make It Rain Campaign returns once more to the Gold Saucer! With a 50% MGP bonus and an assortment of adorable otter-themed goods on offer, you won't want to miss out! Furthermore, campaign attendants will be offering a range of items a
items.content:
The Make It Rain Campaign returns once more to the Gold Saucer! With a 50% MGP bonus and an assortment of adorable otter-themed goods on offer, you won't want to miss out! Furthermore, campaign attendants will be offering a range of items at discounted prices, including some exclusive limited-time deals, so be sure to join the festivities and rake in the rewards while you can!

Event Duration

From Friday, May 29, 2026 at 1:00 a.m. (PDT) / 8:00 (GMT) / 9:00 (BST) / 18:00 (AEST) to Wednesday, June 24, 2026 at 7:59 a.m. (PDT) / 14:59 (GMT) / 15:59 (BST) / Thursday, 25 June at 0:59 (AEST)

How to Participate

Begin the quest "You Otter Be There" by speaking to Ollier in Ul’dah - Steps of Nald (X:9.4 Y:9.1). * Players must be level 15 and above to start the quest. * Players must first complete the quest "It Could Happen to You."

Rewards

* MGP rewards from the Gold Saucer have been increased by 50% for the duration of this event. * Certain items available during previous iterations of the event can be obtained via the campaign attendant. * The Crystal Tower striker, Monster Toss machine, Cuff-a-Cur machine, and a number of other prizes may be purchased at a reduced MGP cost from the campaign attendant. Visit the special site for more details.

items.title: Play Free for up to 4 Days in the Latest Free Login Campaign!
items.summary: In the hope that would-be adventurers can experience all that FFXIV has to offer, we are pleased to announce the latest Free Login Campaign. Follow the Trail to the Heavens ! In Patch 7.5, you can face Enuo in the Unmaking, take a stroll th
items.content:
In the hope that would-be adventurers can experience all that FFXIV has to offer, we are pleased to announce the latest Free Login Campaign. Follow the Trail to the Heavens! In Patch 7.5, you can face Enuo in the Unmaking, take a stroll through Windurst: The Third Walk in the FINAL FANTASY XI alliance raid crossover, and leap into battle in Archeia Harmonias, an all-new arena for Crystalline Conflict. A host of other additions and adjustments will make your time in Eorzea more streamlined and enjoyable, including increased furnishing placement limits, the consolidation of dyes, and additional pieces of gear that can now be converted to outfit glamour or stored in the armoire. With the introduction of Patch 7.5, the Shadowbringers expansion can now be played as part of the FINAL FANTASY XIV Starter Edition. The expansion will be automatically registered to your account if you already own the Starter Edition, so why not take this opportunity to jump into the adventure?

If you or any of your friends are currently taking a break from your adventures, you won't want to miss out on this limited-time event! Visit the Free Login Campaign website for details.

items.title: Final Fantasy XIV Evercold's prelude picks up right where Endwalker left off
items.summary: The shards beg to be whole once more.
items.content:
With our adventures in Tural and the Ninth coming to a close, it’s about time the Warrior of Light and friends return East for their next adventure… which is already in progress, as Voidsent have started appearing all over the globe, particularly in areas where the veil between the Source and the Void is thin. This means Alphinaud and Alisae already have their hands full in Garlemald, and could probably use some help. As things unfold and horrifying secrets come to light, there’s only one thing that’s certain: it’s time to get the band back together.

Final Fantasy XIV’s Patch 7.5, Trail to the Heavens, gave me a bit of whiplash in the best way. It almost immediately brings us back to the pre-Dawntrail plot, following up on Zero, the void, and the fate of the Star. It’s not like Dawntrail didn’t happen, the Key as well as Helmarut, Calyx, and the rest of the Winterers are core to the upcoming story, but we’ve left the more lackadaisical pace behind. I really enjoyed Dawntrail, but its story and pacing were very messy, so this feels like a return to form. The Scions are important again, and while I’m still wary of their characters not really having anywhere else to go in the future, it is genuinely nice to have the gang back together. Part of me is hoping Evercold will pull a bait and switch, focusing on the less developed Scions like Hoary Boulder and crew.

The story here is also pulling directly on threads previously left dangling, giving our actions in Endwalker a bit more weight and consequence. I like Endwalker being the optimistic story it is, and immediately following it with the revelations we see in this patch would have taken some of the wind out of its sails, so having a buffer of a few years helps these twists land much better. I’m also still thoroughly impressed by the cinematography in every cutscene, with most being fully voice acted once again. It feels like the game finally has the budget it deserves, as the team has had the talent to execute on all this for a long time.

Speaking of voice acting: the finale of the Final Fantasy XI crossover raid series continues its great trend of having the characters voiced within the raid itself. It’s incredibly helpful being able to listen to the dialogue instead of just reading it while trying to dodge AoEs and other mechanics. The raid itself is a blast, with some great fights, though I am getting a bit tired of the general FFXIV pattern of mob fight -> boss in an arena -> mob fight and so on. The final boss of the raid mixed things up really well, with a fight that spans two platforms stacked vertically you need to move between occasionally. It’s nothing we haven’t seen an equivalent of before, but just having that bit of creative movement mixes things up in a nice way.

The raid series also reminds me I really need to go back and actually play through XI. I enjoyed what I played a few years ago and I actually felt very nostalgic seeing my home city of Windurst again here, I just need to find the time; maybe if it was ported to modern consoles? I’ll probably write about it here on GamingTrend too, so stay tuned for that.

In terms of other playable content, the new dungeon, the Clyteum, is enjoyable if a bit bland (other than Chort. We love Chort. Straight up Chorting it in the Clyteum). The visuals and setting are nice, but none of the fights really stand out and it’s suffering from fatigue of the typical encounter pattern we see in most every dungeon. The new Trial, The Unmaking, is likewise decent but forgettable, it’s pretty much a footnote in the patch’s story too. 7.5 is definitely more focused on setting things up for the future, which I think is fine for what it is and it certainly does a good job at that.

There’s still more to come for the 7.5X patches, like more Hildebrand quests and another weapon step, and I’ll definitely be there for both of those. I’ve been on again and off again with Final Fantasy XIV since I finished Endwalker, it just felt like a good time for an extended break. I’ve come back for every major patch and occasionally when I felt the itch, but it wasn’t until now that I felt ready to dive back in again. Maybe it’s just Evercold hype, but I’m glad to have the game back in my life again.

items.title: The Maiden's Rhapsody Returns!
items.summary: Experience the intriguing tale bridging Hydaelyn and Vana'diel as The Maiden's Rhapsody─a past collaboration with FFXI─returns to the realm once more! Embark on a journey with one of Vana'diel's heroes and discover what twists of fate broug
items.content:
Experience the intriguing tale bridging Hydaelyn and Vana'diel as The Maiden's Rhapsody─a past collaboration with FFXI─returns to the realm once more! Embark on a journey with one of Vana'diel's heroes and discover what twists of fate brought her to our realm. * This event is identical to the one previously held. * If you have already completed the event, you can replay the quests via the seasonal event replay feature. We highly recommend learning more about this feature on the collaboration special site.

Event Duration

From Friday, May 8, 2026 at 1:00 a.m. (PDT) / 8:00 (GMT) / 9:00 (BST) / 18:00 (AEST) to Monday, May 25, 2026 at 7:59 a.m. (PDT) / 14:59 (GMT) / 15:59 (BST) / Tuesday, 26 May at 00:59 (AEST)

How to Participate

Begin the quest "A Journey to Remember" by speaking to the Remumu in Limsa Lominsa - Upper Decks (X:11.6 Y:11.2).

Rewards

Visit the special site for more details.

items.title: Free trial now to include FINAL FANTASY XIV: Shadowbringers!
items.summary: Beginning Tuesday, April 28, with the release of Patch 7.5, the free trial will also include the award-winning FINAL FANTASY XIV: Shadowbringers. Spread the word and tell your friends, because there’s never been a better time to begin their
items.content: Beginning Tuesday, April 28, with the release of Patch 7.5, the free trial will also include the award-winning FINAL FANTASY XIV: Shadowbringers. Spread the word and tell your friends, because there’s never been a better time to begin their adventure! Check out the free trial. For those who have already registered FINAL FANTASY XIV: A Realm Reborn to their accounts, worry not─this change also applies to all existing owners as well. * Service accounts will be updated to reflect this change starting on Tuesday, April 28. Please note that the registration status update may not be displayed immediately on the Mog Station.
